trajectory_summary: Carter had a warm, motivated customer and a clean verify run, but the call ends in a conditional callback rather than a locked appointment. The bill-access problem surfaced at 01:32 and was never solved — it became the gating condition that left the slot unbooked. The button-up was stripped of every structural anchor the codex requires: no locked time, no 'is that fair' handshake, no computer/no-driving instruction, no one-hour block stated.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown' — the correct classification is 'conditional_callback / tentative_appointment.' A time was verbally negotiated (9AM John's time / 8AM Carter's time, tomorrow morning) but Carter explicitly stated 'I'm not able to put you in the schedule until I get that bill,' meaning no slot was locked. This is a tentative appointment with meaningful no-show risk: bill not received, customer traveling internationally for ~1 week, credit softly confirmed ('I think yes'), and no button-up anchors executed.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Conditional slot-lock + button-up execution on tentative appointments
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I'm not able to put you in the schedule until I get that. So I'll just shoot you a text.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Carter had a warm, willing customer and a verbally negotiated time, then left the call with zero button-up anchors and no locked slot. On a paid lead, the bill requirement before booking is codex-compliant — the coaching gap is not the bill requirement itself but the absence of conditional slot-locking language and any button-up element. With John traveling internationally for ~1 week, every day without a locked slot and a confirmation text is a day the appointment cools. Teach Carter the conditional slot-lock: 'I've got 9AM your time penciled in — the moment I get the bill, you're confirmed. Is that fair?' That single move executes a time anchor, a text promise, and a verbal handshake without violating the paid-lead rule.

key_moments.quote: Because of obvious reasons, you know, for the economy and for the climate and, we're saving some money.
key_moments.stewart_read: John offered a genuine, three-part motivation unprompted — economy, climate, savings. The codex flags this as the emotional anchor of the entire pitch and explicitly grades depth: 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Bottom setters check the box and move on.' Carter responded with 'Perfect. Yeah.' and pivoted immediately to verify. The motivation was captured but not deepened — no follow-up on which of the three mattered most, no bill-pain bridge, no curiosity creation off the savings angle.

key_moments.quote: I'm out of town. I'm in Mexico. So I need to look that up.
key_moments.stewart_read: John's travel disclosure is a process signal, not an empathy moment. The codex's shopping_customer_protocol is explicit: 'Walk customer through utility website on phone, capture via screenshot text.' Carter acknowledged the travel context for scheduling (time zone negotiation at 03:02–03:36) but never asked the pivotal question: 'Do you have online access to your utility account from your phone?' John said 'I probably could' at 03:46 — hedged, not definitive — which suggests the path was available but untested. Carter accepted the uncertainty rather than solving it.

key_moments.quote: One of the main requirements for getting solar in Colorado is having a credit score above 650. Is that the case for you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Two issues compound here. First, the codex threshold is 670; Carter stated 650 — a schema drift that has appeared in prior calls (SESSION18_fcbf33c1) and remains unresolved pending Kenny's confirmation. Second, John's response at 02:00 — 'I think yes' — is a soft, unconfirmed qualification. Spencer's operator note in the codex is directly applicable: customers self-report Vantage scores from credit-card apps, not the FICO bureau Ion pulls; a stated 'I think yes' at 650 (or 670) may or may not survive the closer's actual credit pull. Carter accepted the hedge and moved on without probing.

key_moments.quote: For about a week, and I'll be traveling. So
key_moments.stewart_read: John's 'about a week' travel disclosure compounds the no-show risk beyond the Mexico-today context. The codex's soft_outcome_distinction directive is explicit: 'Callbacks WITH locked times vs we'll touch base — major delta on no-show rate.' Carter negotiated a time (9AM John's time / 8AM Carter's time) but then explicitly declined to lock the slot at 04:02, pending bill receipt. The result is a callback promise — not a locked appointment — against a week of travel disruption, with an unconfirmed credit qualification and no bill in hand.
